behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler↔signature verifier↔queue handoff (not just pure hash math). A unit test of HMAC alone can miss wiring bugs like using parsed JSON instead of raw body. Real OS/network/LLM are not required. Rejected System: behavior text only requires 401 + no forward, not real network faults/timeouts/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the HTTP handler in-process with real middleware wiring, send a request with a tampered raw body/signature pair, assert 401, and assert queue write/forward method was not called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across handler and persistent queue modules; mocks can hide sequencing/transaction bugs. Needs real local persistence wiring, but not real external network/subprocess/LLM. Rejected System: behavior does not require real network conditions, only ordering between local components.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process server + real persistent queue backend (local DB/filesystem). Instrument queue write completion and response send; assert write commits before 200 is observed.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Validates decoupling between request path and async worker path across modules. A pure unit test can miss framework wiring where await accidentally blocks response. No real OS/network/subprocess/LLM dependency required. Rejected System: no requirement for real network failure modes; in-process HTTP execution is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Start handler in-process with downstream processor intentionally delayed; send valid event; assert response returns before processor resolves and event was queued for later processing.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on wall-clock timing under concurrency, which is sensitive to real HTTP server/event-loop scheduling and contention. In-process mocks/fake timers can hide true latency and queueing effects. No LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service as a real process with real HTTP requests (concurrent load), keep downstream workers busy with slow prior events, then measure end-to-end response latency for new webhook and assert p95/each response <= 3s per requirement.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is at module/storage boundary (handler + persistence uniqueness/idempotency). Unit logic alone can miss real duplicate-write behavior. No real OS/network/subprocess/LLM needed. Rejected System: behavior text is about duplicate persistence outcome, not network-level phenomena.
behaviors.test_description: With real local persistent queue storage, submit same event ID twice through handler and assert only one queue record exists (or second write is rejected and not persisted).

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Tests contract mapping between queue backpressure signal and HTTP response semantics across modules. Mocking too much can hide translation bugs, but real OS/network/subprocess are unnecessary. Rejected System: requirement is status/header mapping, not real network behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Configure queue to full state (or use real tiny-capacity queue at limit), send valid webhook, assert 503 and required Retry-After header, and assert no extra enqueue occurs.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure boundary is queue component + persistence/config wiring; needs real local backend behavior rather than pure in-memory logic. No real external OS/network/subprocess dependency beyond local storage; no LLM. Rejected System: behavior does not require external process/network conditions, only local persistence enforcement.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate queue with a fixed capacity using real persistence backend, fill to limit, attempt one additional enqueue, and assert rejection plus unchanged persisted depth.
